perturbation.original_text: 1.2 "Applicable Law" means all laws, ordinances, rules, rulings, directives and regulations of any Governmental Authority that apply to the development, manufacture or supply of any Product or the other activities contemplated under this Supply Agreement, including (i) all applicable federal, state and local laws, rules and regulations; (ii) the U.S. Federal Food, Drug and Cosmetic Act; (iii) regulations and guidelines of the FDA and other Regulatory Authorities, including cGMPs, if applicable; and (iv) any applicable non-U.S. equivalents of any of the foregoing, including guidelines of the International Conference on Harmonization of Technical Requirements for Registration of Pharmaceuticals for Human Use (as amended from time to time).
perturbation.changed_text: 1.2 "Applicable Law" means only those rulings explicitly mentioned within this agreement. For all matters outside of this document, the term should not be considered to be a constraint upon the manufacturer or supplier.
perturbation.explanation: This change drastically narrows the definition of 'Applicable Law', effectively stating that only rulings named in this contract apply, and that the manufacturer or supplier does not need to follow outside laws. This contradicts the broad interpretation of 'Applicable Law' generally understood in legal contexts and regulatory compliance.
perturbation.contradicted_law: U.S. Federal Food, Drug and Cosmetic Act
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text includes the U.S. Federal Food, Drug and Cosmetic Act, which defines adulteration and misbranding. The modified text excludes it, therefore allowing the manufacturer to violate federal law without violating the contract.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: U.S.C. Title 21 - FOOD AND DRUGS 21 U.S.C. United States Code, 2011 Edition Title 21 - FOOD AND DRUGS CHAPTER 9 - FEDERAL FOOD, DRUG, AND COSMETIC ACT From the U.S. Government Publishing Office, www.gpo.gov CHAPTER 9—FEDERAL FOOD, DRUG, AND COSMETIC ACT SUBCHAPTER I—SHORT TITLE Sec. 301. Short title. SUBCHAPTER II—DEFINITIONS 321. Definitions; generally. 321a. “Butter” defined. 321b. “Package” defined. 321c. Nonfat dry milk; “milk” defined. 321d. Market names for catfish and ginseng. SUBCHAPTER III—PROHIBITED ACTS AND PENALTIES 331. Prohibited acts. 332. Injunction proceedings. 333. Penalties. 333a. Repealed. 334. Seizure. 335. Hearing before report of criminal violation. 335a. Debarment, temporary denial of approval, and suspension. 335b. Civil penalties. 335c. Authority to withdraw approval of abbreviated drug applications. 336. Report of minor violations. 337. Proceedings in name of United States; provision as to subpoenas. SUBCHAPTER IV—FOOD 341. Definitions and standards for food. 342. Adulterated food. 343. Misbranded food. 343–1. National uniform nutrition labeling. 343–2. Dietary supplement labeling exemptions. 343–3. Disclosure. 343a. Repealed. 344. Emergency permit control. 345. Regulations making exemptions. 346. Tolerances for poisonous or deleterious substances in food; regulations. 346a. Tolerances and exemptions for pesticide chemical residues. 346b. Authorization of appropriations. 347. Intrastate sales of colored oleomargarine. 347a. Congressional declaration of policy regarding oleomargarine sales. 347b. Contravention of State laws. 348. Food additives. 349. Bottled drinking water standards; publication in Federal Register. 350. Vitamins and minerals. 350a. Infant formulas. 350b. New dietary ingredients. 350c. Maintenance and inspection of records. 350d. Registration of food facilities. 350e. Sanitary transportation practices. 350f. Reportable food registry. 350g. Hazard analysis and risk-based preventive controls. 350h. Standards for produce safety. 350i. Protection against intentional adulteration. 350j. Targeting of inspection resources for domestic facilities, foreign facilities, and ports of entry; annual report. 350k. Laboratory accreditation for analyses of foods. 350 l . Mandatory recall authority. 350 l –1. Annual report to Congress. SUBCHAPTER V—DRUGS AND DEVICES
perturbation.justification: The modified text explicitly limits the definition of 'Applicable Law' to only those rulings mentioned within the agreement, thereby excluding external laws and regulations. This directly contradicts the original text, which includes 'all laws, ordinances, rules, rulings, directives and regulations of any Governmental Authority' and specifically mentions the 'U.S. Federal Food, Drug and Cosmetic Act'. The Law Explanation highlights that excluding this act allows the manufacturer to violate federal law without violating the contract. Scraped Law Snippet 2, titled 'U.S.C. Title 21 - FOOD AND DRUGS,' indicates the existence of a comprehensive set of laws related to food and drugs, which the changed text seeks to circumvent. By narrowing the scope of 'Applicable Law,' the contract becomes non-compliant with legally recognized definitions, aligning with the Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: 1.3 "cGMPs" means current good manufacturing practices and standards as set forth (and as amended from time to time) in the current Good Manufacturing Practice Regulations of the U.S. Code of Federal Regulations, including 21 C.F.R. Sections 210 and 211, the European Community Directive 2003/94/EC and the ICH Harmonised Tripartite Guideline, Good Manufacturing Practice Guides for Active Pharmaceutical Ingredients (Q7).
perturbation.changed_text: 1.3 "cGMPs" means suggested manufacturing guidelines that are not legally binding. The manufacturer is encouraged to follow these guidelines when practical, but deviations are permitted based on business needs.
perturbation.explanation: This change redefines 'cGMPs' (current Good Manufacturing Practices) from legally binding regulations to mere suggestions. This directly contradicts the mandatory nature of cGMPs in pharmaceutical manufacturing.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original contract correctly references 21 CFR Parts 210 and 211 as defining cGMPs. The modified text states that these regulations are merely 'suggested', which directly contradicts their legally binding status as defined by the FDA.

perturbation.justification: The changed text redefines 'cGMPs' from legally binding regulations to 'suggested manufacturing guidelines'. This directly contradicts the mandatory nature of cGMPs as implied by the reference to 21 CFR Parts 210 and 211 in the original text and explicitly stated in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly refers to 21 CFR Part 210 as 'Current Good Manufacturing Practice in Manufacturing, Processing, Packing, or Holding of Drugs; General', suggesting a legally binding nature, not mere suggestions.

perturbation.original_text: 1.17 "Regulatory Authority" means any federal, national, multinational, state, provincial or local regulatory agency, department, bureau or other governmental entity, including the FDA, with authority over the development, Manufacture or commercialization (including approval of Regulatory Approvals) of any Product(s) in any jurisdiction in the Territory.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.17 "Regulatory Authority" means only the internal quality control department of Sutro, which will self-regulate the Product.
perturbation.explanation: This change significantly limits the definition of 'Regulatory Authority' to only the company's internal quality control, excluding external agencies like the FDA. This contradicts the accepted understanding of regulatory oversight in pharmaceutical manufacturing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (FD&C Act)
perturbation.law_explanation: The FD&C Act grants the FDA the authority to regulate the manufacturing of drugs. The modified definition of 'Regulatory Authority' attempts to eliminate the role of external regulatory bodies. Therefore, the contract goes against the FD&C Act.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (FD&C Act) | FDA Skip to main content Skip to FDA Search Skip to in this section menu Skip to footer links In this section : Laws Enforced by FDA Home Regulatory Information Laws Enforced by FDA Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (FD&C Act) Laws Enforced by FDA FD&C Act Reference Information United States Code, Title 21 The laws of the United States are organized by subject into the United States Code. The United States Code contains only the currently enacted statutory language. The official United States Code is maintained by the Office of the Law Revision Counsel in the United States House of Representatives. The Office of the Law Revision Counsel reviews enacted laws and determines where the statutory language should be codified related to its topic. The Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act and subsequent amending statutes are codified into Title 21 Chapter 9 of the United States Code. The listing of FD&C Act sections presented here identifies both the FD&C Act and U.S. Code section numbers, which can be used to narrow your search on the Law Revision Counsel website. To search the FD&C Act on the Law Revision Counsel website, use the Advanced Search to limit the your search to Title 21. To search the FD&C Act on the Law Revision Counsel website, you may either search by U.S. code section number or browse the Title 21 section listing . Content current as of: 03/29/2018 Back to Top
perturbation.justification: The changed text redefines 'Regulatory Authority' to mean only the company's internal quality control, excluding external regulatory agencies like the FDA. This contradicts the FD&C Act, which, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 2, the FDA enforces. The changed text attempts to eliminate the role of external regulatory bodies, creating a clear conflict. This directly undermines the FDA's mandated oversight in drug manufacturing, as the definition of Regulatory Authority now excludes the FDA, violating the intended regulatory framework.
